file_name: XACCT Technologies, Inc.SUPPORT AND MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 "Incident" means a single, discrete, malfunction or other problem which may require more than one (1) response before it is closed.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 "Incident" means any communication, inquiry, or request made by the Licensee to XACCT, regardless of whether it pertains to a malfunction or problem.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of "Incident" broadens the term to include any communication, contradicting the common and legal understanding of an incident as a problem or malfunction. This could lead to disputes about what constitutes a billable incident.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CCRA's implied warranty of service requires that service contracts be clearly defined. Broadening "Incident" to include any communication makes it unclear what services are covered under the maintenance agreement and thus goes against the warranty that the services will meet the needs of the consumer.

(a) This section shall be known and may be cited as the Tanner Consumer Protection Act. (b) It shall be presumed that a reasonable number of attempts have been made to conform a new motor vehicle to the applicable express warranties if, within 18 months from delivery to the buyer or 18,000 miles on the odometer of the vehicle, whichever occurs first, one or more of the following occurs: (1) The same nonconformity results in a condition that is likely to cause death or serious bodily injury if the vehicle is driven and the nonconformity has been subject to repair two or more times by the manufacturer or its agents, and the buyer or lessee has at least once directly notified the manufacturer of the need for the repair of the nonconformity. (2) The same noncon
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Incident' to include 'any communication,' which contradicts the common understanding of an incident as a problem. The Law Explanation states this undermines the 'warranty that the services will meet the needs of the consumer' under the CCRA's implied warranty of service. Snippet 1 mentions 'CIV 1793.22' which is the 'Tanner Consumer Protection Act.' Although the snippet focuses on motor vehicles, the overall context indicates consumer protection laws require clearly defined service contracts, and broadening 'Incident' creates ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: Fees shall be payable within thirty (30) days of invoice which shall be exclusive of any applicable local, state, federal, use, excise, value added, GST or other taxes imposed on the fees payable for the Product Maintenance and Support Services which shall be the responsibility of Licensee.
perturbation.changed_text: Fees shall be payable within ninety (90) days of invoice which shall be inclusive of all applicable local, state, federal, use, excise, value added, GST or other taxes imposed on the fees payable for the Product Maintenance and Support Services which shall be the responsibility of XACCT.
perturbation.explanation: This modification shifts the responsibility for paying taxes from the Licensee to XACCT and includes taxes in the initial fee, contradicting standard business practices and potentially violating tax laws if not properly accounted for. The extension of the payable days can also cause issues related to accounting and auditing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 6041 of the IRC requires businesses to report payments of income, including services. By stating XACCT is responsible for taxes on fees which are explicitly the responsibility of the Licensee under standard practices, the agreement creates potential misreporting of income and tax liabilities.

Information at source (a) Payments of $600 or more All persons engaged in a trade or business and making payment in the course of such trade or business to another person, of rent, salaries, wages, premiums, annuities, compensations, remunerations, emoluments,
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts standard tax practices and introduces potential misreporting issues, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Specifically, by shifting the tax responsibility from the Licensee to XACCT and including taxes in the fee, the agreement creates an inconsistency in how income and tax liabilities are reported. The LLM explanation references section 6041 of the IRC, and Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites 26 USC 6041, which concerns information reporting at the source for payments of $600 or more in trade or business. This section implies that the party making the payment (originally the Licensee) should report the payment. By changing the terms so that XACCT is now responsible for the taxes on fees that should be the Licensee's responsibility, it creates a situation where the reporting may not accurately reflect the initial transaction and potential income.

perturbation.original_text: 4.3 THE TERMS OF THE LICENSE AGREEMENT PERTAINING TO LIMITED WARRANTY, DISCLAIMERS OF WARRANTY AND LIMITATION OF LIABILITY SHALL APPLY TO THE MAJOR AND MINOR RELEASES OF PRODUCT DELIVERED ACCORDING TO THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 XACCT offers all Major and Minor Releases without any warranty, expressed or implied, and assumes no liability for any damages arising from the use of these releases.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates all warranties and liabilities for Major and Minor Releases, which contradicts the typical inclusion of at least a limited warranty in software agreements. This directly opposes consumer protection laws that ensure products are fit for purpose and free from defects.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the California Commercial Code states that goods sold must be merchantable, meaning they are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. By disclaiming all warranties, XACCT attempts to circumvent this implied warranty, which is generally not permissible without explicit and conspicuous waivers that meet legal standards.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted b
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original contract by eliminating all warranties and liabilities for Major and Minor Releases. This conflicts with the implied warranty of merchantability under California Commercial Code COM 2314 (1), which states that goods must be merchantable unless 'excluded or modified (Section 2316).' While exclusion is possible, the Perturbation Explanation notes this directly opposes consumer protection laws, and the Law Explanation suggests that waivers must be explicit and conspicuous. The altered text attempts to remove all warranties, including the implied warranty of merchantability, which is a contradiction.
